project_overview.project_name: Bolivia Improving Sustainable Access to Electricity Project (IDTR III)
project_overview.project_development_objective: Expand and improve access to sustainable electricity services in rural areas of Bolivia
project_overview.beneficiaries: 140,000 people with new or improved electricity services; over 200,000 beneficiaries of communication and behavior campaigns

geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: no - Bolivia is not classified as fragile or conflict-affected, though it faces governance and macroeconomic challenges

key_components.component_name: Component 1: Expand and improve access to sustainable electricity services in rural areas
key_components.description: Grid extensions for households (25,000 connections), grid extensions for productive uses (350 connections), standalone PV systems for households (9,000 systems) and public institutions (300 systems), and minigrid systems (6 systems) powered by renewable energy
key_components.financing: US$132.00 million (US$110.00 million IBRD, US$6.00 million beneficiaries, US$16.00 million others)

key_components.description: Policy support for just energy transition including analytical work on energy efficiency, climate resilience, and decarbonization; strengthening distribution sector capacities through training and knowledge outputs

Project management, coordination, financial management, environmental and social management, monitoring and evaluation, stakeholder engagement, grievance redress mechanisms, and behavior change campaigns
key_components.financing: US$5.69 million (US$4.69 million IBRD, US$1.00 million GoB)

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: Programa de Electricidad para Vivir con Dignidad (PEVD) within the Viceministerio de Electricidad y Energías Renovables (VMEER) of the Ministry of Hydrocarbons and Energies (MHE), coordinated with Empresa Nacional de Electricidad (ENDE)
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: Project Implementation Units (PIUs) in PEVD and ENDE; collaboration agreements with Departmental Autonomous Governments (GADs) and Municipal Autonomous Governments (GAMs); O&M&R contracts with distribution companies; coordination with other development partners (IDB, CAF, GIZ, KfW)
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: World Bank Procurement Regulations for IPF Borrowers (5th edition, September 2023); open international competitive bidding for large contracts; open national competitive bidding for small contracts; direct selection for consulting services when justified
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: Semi-annual interim financial reports; annual project financial statements; annual audit reports; M&E system with GEMS technology; mid-term review by 2027; baseline, midterm, and implementation assessments; tracking of PDO and intermediate indicators

identified_risks.risk_description: Presidential elections scheduled for 2025 may shift sector and geographical priorities; changes in authorities could affect project teams and cause implementation delays
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Provision of basic services expected to remain priority across political spectrum; capacity building for key staff to communicate project benefits to new authorities; flexibility in project design to adapt to changing administrations

identified_risks.risk_description: High fiscal deficit (7.1% of GDP in 2022) limits fiscal space; low international reserves (3.1 months of imports) could complicate programming and make inputs expensive or difficult to access
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Direct procurement by ENDE regional subsidiaries for grid extension goods; direct payments to suppliers for PV and minigrid systems; integration into national budget and PFM systems

identified_risks.risk_category: institutional capacity for implementation and sustainability
identified_risks.risk_description: Limited capacity of implementing entities to manage simultaneous processes; need for coordination between PEVD and ENDE PIUs; coordination challenges with GAMs, GADs, and beneficiaries; staff rotation risks
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Building on experience from IDTR I, GPOBA, IDTR II projects; capacity strengthening through training; development of clear implementation arrangements and operational manual; hiring of qualified staff with annual contracts; communications strategy to facilitate coordination

identified_risks.risk_description: Magnitude of operation, large-scale decentralized interventions, high dispersion of works, remote locations with difficult access, limited FM and procurement knowledge among PEVD and ENDE staff, high staff turnover, nature of transactions (high number of geographically dispersed contracts)
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Increasing fiduciary staff with relevant experience; training on Bank Procurement Regulations; preparation of comprehensive Operational Manual; separate Designated Accounts for PEVD and ENDE; use of VISUAL and ENDESIS financial systems; semi-annual financial reporting; annual independent audits; Bank supervision missions

identified_risks.risk_description: National scope including protected areas; limited social information available; potential temporary or permanent restrictions on land use; borrower's weak experience managing E&S risks per ESF standards; potential elite capture and exclusion of vulnerable populations; labor influx risks; local tensions and conflicts; forced labor risks in solar panel supply chain; GBV risks
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Environmental and Social Management Framework (ESMF); Indigenous Peoples Planning Framework (IPPF); Resettlement Policy Framework (RPF); Stakeholder Engagement Plan (SEP); Labor Management Plan (LMP); Biodiversity Management Plans for protected areas; GBV action plan and codes of conduct; forced labor bidder declarations and strengthened procurement provisions for solar panels; dedicated E&S specialists in PIUs; training on ESF standards; grievance redress mechanism

identified_risks.risk_description: Need to assess tariff schemes and sustainability of electricity sector entities; potential future challenges from fuel subsidies
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Component 2 activities to assess tariff schemes and sector sustainability; policy support for just energy transition

identified_risks.risk_description: Complexity of deploying multiple technological solutions across dispersed rural areas; need to ensure quality and sustainability of installations
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Use of proven technologies from previous projects; technical expertise of PEVD and ENDE; training and capacity building; quality supervision; reliance on distribution companies for O&M&R

identified_risks.risk_description: Risk of inadequate stakeholder engagement leading to project resistance or elite capture; need for inclusive consultation processes
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Comprehensive Stakeholder Engagement Plan; grievance redress mechanism; targeted strategies for vulnerable populations; women's participation in subproject design (10% target); communication campaigns; mid-term review to assess stakeholder satisfaction

triggered_safeguard_policies: ESS 1: Assessment and Management of Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts
ESS 3: Resource Efficiency and Pollution Prevention and Management
ESS 5: Land Acquisition, Restrictions on Land Use and Involuntary Resettlement
ESS 6: Biodiversity Conservation and Sustainable Management of Living Natural Resources
ESS 7: Indigenous Peoples/Sub-Saharan African Historically Underserved Traditional Local Communities
ESS 10: Stakeholder Engagement and Information Disclosure
safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: yes - Project-level grievance redress mechanism established; World Bank Grievance Redress Service (GRS) available; World Bank Accountability Mechanism (Inspection Panel and Dispute Resolution Service) available; separate handling of GBV cases with binding codes of conduct
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Gender Action Plan prepared addressing: (1) women's double workload (productive-reproductive role) through training on shared domestic responsibilities; (2) women's participation in energy sector through diagnosis, training, and hiring targets (30% women in Component 3 teams, 10% in subproject design); (3) GBV prevention through leaflets with critical path information, codes of conduct, awareness training; measurement of change in time women spend on domestic/unpaid care work (target: 7% reduction); target of 500 women establishing local businesses/microenterprises; 1,000 women trained in technical electricity and renewable energy issues
vulnerable_groups: Women (disproportionately vulnerable with less access to energy services)

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Bolivia is not classified as fragile or conflict-affected. However, the project incorporates conflict sensitivity measures: (1) stakeholder engagement processes designed to prevent local tensions and conflicts; (2) inclusive consultation to avoid elite capture; (3) targeted strategies for vulnerable and underrepresented groups; (4) grievance redress mechanisms to address concerns; (5) community involvement in subproject preparation and implementation; (6) communication campaigns to build understanding and support
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: Bolivia faces governance and macroeconomic fragility factors: (1) high fiscal deficit (7.1% of GDP) limiting government capacity; (2) low international reserves (3.1 months of imports); (3) political instability with elections in 2025; (4) staff turnover and institutional capacity constraints; (5) limited experience with World Bank ESF standards; (6) decentralized governance requiring coordination across multiple levels; (7) vulnerability to climate change (Global Climate Risk Index 2021 identified Bolivia as one of ten most affected countries); (8) poverty concentration in rural areas (32% rural poverty vs 15.2% national); (9) high inequality and service delivery gaps
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: Project activities in remote rural areas with difficult access; labor influx risks from civil works; potential communicable disease risks; road safety risks from traffic alterations; occupational health and safety risks from working at heights and with high voltage equipment; GBV risks in context of high incidence in Bolivia; forced labor risks in solar panel supply chain; potential security challenges during construction in dispersed locations
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: no - Project does not involve large-scale displacement. However, potential minor impacts: (1) temporary access restrictions during construction; (2) temporary or permanent restrictions on land use for infrastructure placement; (3) opportunity costs for local populations if land used for energy systems has alternative economic/social uses; (4) Resettlement Policy Framework (RPF) prepared to address any land acquisition or involuntary resettlement needs; (5) Environmental and Social Management Framework includes procedures for managing land-related impacts
